summary: Boundaries are well-enumerated, but several production-grade controls are asserted without a defined mechanism: the renderer→worker loopback API has no authn/authz scheme (the most load-bearing gap), the provider-matrix↔egress-gate composition rule for Employer-Work is unspecified, the PRD-required prompt-injection SCANNER is absent from the architecture, and Telegram + Drive/NotebookLM raw-content egress paths are not bound to egressPolicy.

findings.finding: The renderer→loopback worker API (tRPC + WS/SSE event stream) has no defined authentication/authorization mechanism. THREAT_MODEL.md lists 'origin/session checks' as a control, but ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §5/§10 only specify 'loopback binding' + 'typed tRPC' — and loopback is NOT an authorization boundary. Any local process, and any web page the owner visits (via localhost fetch / DNS-rebinding / unguarded WS+SSE cross-origin), can call the worker API, which is the single gateway to raw workspace data, the egress gate, approvals, and command execution. The PRD also never defines this auth (only §17 'loopback-only'). Under production-grade posture this is the boundary protecting everything behind the worker.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §5 'Worker API binds loopback only' (line 174) and §10 'Worker API is loopback-only' (line 290); THREAT_MODEL.md row 'Renderer -> loopback worker API ... origin/session checks' (line 22); PRD §17 Network exposure (line 1007) defines only loopback binding, no API auth
findings.proposed_fix: Specify a concrete renderer↔worker auth scheme in the final ARCHITECTURE.md §5/§10: e.g., a per-launch session token minted by Electron main and injected into the renderer only via preload (never discoverable by other localhost clients), required on every tRPC call AND on the WS/SSE handshake; strict Origin/Sec-Fetch validation on the event stream; and ideally a Unix domain socket or randomized port+token rather than a fixed loopback TCP port. Add an acceptance test that a same-machine non-renderer client and a cross-origin browser page are both rejected.
findings.anchor: §5 Policy, Security, and Egress; §10 Local App API

findings.finding: The composition rule between ProviderMatrix.allowedProviders and the Employer-Work egress gate is unspecified, leaving open the audit question 'can the provider matrix bypass the Employer-Work egress gate?'. §5 lists 'workspace/capability matrix' and 'egress policy' as parallel controls but never states precedence: that when Employer Work raw-content egress acknowledgment is OFF, the Runtime Broker MUST route only to local zero-egress providers (or refuse the job), regardless of which cloud providers are 'allowed' in the matrix. PRD §16.5 is explicit that zero-egress requires a local-only model and that the employer meeting-closeout DoD branch is gated on the ack being ON — implying a hard routing rule the architecture does not pin.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §5 lines 165-168 and §7 lines 219-221 list matrix + egress as separate constraints with no ordering; PRD §16.5 (line 995) 'Enforcement is at the Tool Gateway / Agent Runtime Broker before content leaves the device ... zero-egress operation would require a local-only model'; threat-model row 'Worker -> provider/cloud' (line 23)
findings.proposed_fix: State the rule explicitly: egress policy is evaluated AFTER provider selection and is a veto — for an Employer Work AgentJob carrying raw content with egress ack=off, the Broker may select ONLY a loopback local provider; if none is available/conformant the job fails closed (no cloud fallback). Add a test that an Employer-Work raw-content job with ack=off and only cloud providers allowed is rejected, not silently downgraded.
findings.anchor: §5 Policy, Security, and Egress; §7 Provider and Runtime Broker

findings.finding: The PRD makes a passed prompt-injection SCAN a precondition for automatic Markdown writes (§16.1 'prompt-injection scanning and policy gates'; §16.2 auto-write requires 'content has PASSED prompt-injection scanning'; Appendix-A A5 'injection-scan passed'). The architecture draft and threat model implement injection defense ONLY via ING-7 tool-stripping + JSON-schema gates — there is no named injection-scanning/detection component anywhere in §5, §6, §7, or §12. Tool-stripping prevents direct side effects but does not satisfy the PRD's scan-gated auto-write condition.
findings.evidence: PRD §16.1 (line 954), §16.2 auto-write row (line 969), Appendix-A A5 (line 1214); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §5 lines 167-168 and §6/§7 rely on tool-stripping + schema only; THREAT_MODEL.md prompt-injection row (line 35) mitigations omit any scanner
findings.proposed_fix: Add an explicit injection-scanning step to the final ARCHITECTURE.md (in §5 policy or §6 KnowledgeWriter precondition path): name where the scan runs (on untrusted source content and/or on the KnowledgeMutationPlan before auto-apply), what a failure does (block auto-write → route to approval / System Health review), and cite it as the implementation of the §16.2 auto-write precondition. Include it in the §12 prompt-injection corpus acceptance.
findings.anchor: §5 Policy, Security, and Egress; §6 KnowledgeWriter rules

findings.finding: Telegram is a third-party processor that RECEIVES outbound content (brief text, meeting-prep, approval-card payloads that can carry Employer-Work action details), yet it is absent from the egress policy. PRD §16.5 names permitted processors as only the cloud-LLM runtime + mapped Drive/NotebookLM; the threat model lists Telegram as an external write target but has NO trust-boundary row for worker→Telegram egress. With Employer-Work raw-content egress off by default, nothing in the architecture stops a brief/approval payload from leaking Employer-Work content to Telegram's servers.
findings.evidence: THREAT_MODEL.md Assets line 15 lists Telegram as a write target but Trust Boundaries table (lines 21-29) has no Telegram row; PRD §16.5 egress processors list (line 995) excludes Telegram; PRD Telegram channel §8.2 (line 426) and DoD (line 1028) describe content delivery; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §8/§11 do not gate Telegram by egressPolicy
findings.proposed_fix: Add a worker→Telegram trust-boundary row and bring Telegram under egressPolicy in the final ARCHITECTURE.md: classify what content classes may be sent to Telegram per workspace, enforce that Employer-Work raw/identifying content to Telegram obeys the same egress acknowledgment, and prefer sanitized/metadata-only payloads for Employer Work when ack=off. Add a leakage test for the Telegram channel.
findings.anchor: §5 Policy, Security, and Egress; §8 Connector and Tool Gateways

findings.finding: The Drive-backed NotebookLM managed-doc path uploads raw workspace content to Google Drive (a named egress processor in §16.5), but the architecture does not tie that upload to the egress gate. §8 'Notebook' and the Tool Gateway describe Drive/Docs writes without stating that an Employer-Work raw-content upload to Drive is blocked when egress ack=off. The egress gate appears centered on the provider/Runtime-Broker path, leaving the Connector/Tool-Gateway raw-content egress (Drive upload for source packs) as an unguarded second egress route.
findings.evidence: PRD §16.5 names 'mapped Google Drive / NotebookLM' as egress processors (line 995); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §8 Notebook lines 250-253 and Tool Gateway lines 244-249 do not reference egressPolicy for Drive uploads; threat model has no Drive-upload egress row
findings.proposed_fix: Make egress enforcement a single chokepoint covering BOTH the Runtime Broker (provider prompts) and the Tool/Connector Gateway (Drive/NotebookLM uploads). Explicitly state in §5/§8 that any raw-content upload to Drive for Employer Work is subject to the same egress acknowledgment, and add a Drive-upload egress acceptance test.
findings.anchor: §5 Policy, Security, and Egress; §8 Connector and Tool Gateways

findings.finding: The 'no remote tunnel' guarantee for local providers (the basis for zero-egress Employer-Work processing) is asserted but has no runtime enforcement mechanism. §5 and the threat model say 'local endpoint allowlist, no remote tunnel' and rely on 'conformance tests', but a conformance test cannot detect that an Ollama/LM Studio endpoint at localhost is itself proxying to a remote backend. If the user's local server is misconfigured/tunneled, content the system believes is zero-egress leaves the device while the System Health egress state still reads 'off'.
findings.evidence: THREAT_MODEL.md 'Worker -> local provider' row controls 'local endpoint allowlist, conformance tests, no remote tunnel' (line 24) and threat row line 43; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §5 line 180; PRD §16.5 note 'zero-egress operation would require a local-only model' (line 995)
findings.proposed_fix: Specify the enforceable parts in §5/§7: require local-provider endpoints to resolve to a loopback/link-local address (reject non-loopback hosts for zero-egress routing), and document the residual limitation that zero-egress correctness still depends on the user's local server not being remotely backed (surface this caveat in System Health rather than claiming a hard guarantee). Flag the residual as a known limitation.
findings.anchor: §5 Policy, Security, and Egress; §7 Provider and Runtime Broker

findings.finding: The workspace→GCL projection boundary treats sanitization as a tagging/schema concern but does not address that sanitized summaries are MODEL-GENERATED from potentially injected workspace content — making projection generation itself a prompt-injection-exposed boundary crossing. A transcript/source injection could steer a 'sanitized' Employer-Work summary to embed raw/identifying details that then land in the cross-workspace GCL/global brief. The threat model's GCL row cites 'visibility levels, projection schema, tests' but not output-leakage validation or ING-7 read-only execution for the projection-generating agent.
findings.evidence: THREAT_MODEL.md 'Workspace -> GCL' row (line 25) lists only visibility levels/schema/tests; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §6 GCL rules lines 207-212 describe stored fields but not injection-safe generation; PRD WS-7 leakage suite (line 1136) and §11.4 cover brief leakage but the GCL-projection generation step is not called out in the draft
findings.proposed_fix: State in §6 that GCL projection generation runs under an ING-7 read-only toolPolicy and that its OUTPUT (the sanitized payload) is schema-validated and leakage-checked before crossing into the GCL store/global brief, not just tagged with a visibility level. Extend the WS-7 leakage suite to adversarially target projection generation.
findings.anchor: §6 Knowledge ... and GCL; §5 Policy, Security, and Egress

findings.finding: Outbound secret/log handling is under-specified relative to the threat model. The threat model lists 'log redaction' and 'no secrets in renderer' as controls, but the architecture has no observability/logging section defining redaction, and the secret-scan is described only on the INBOUND KnowledgeWriter→Markdown path — there is no scan/redaction on content sent OUTBOUND to cloud providers (Markdown context can contain credential-shaped strings forwarded in prompts).
findings.evidence: THREAT_MODEL.md secrets row 'Keychain, secret scan, log redaction, .env non-secret only' (line 41); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §6 secret scan is on KnowledgeWriter commit (line 197); no logging/redaction section exists; PRD §16.1 secret scan is on Markdown mutation/commit (lines 877, 959)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a short observability/secret-handling note to the final ARCHITECTURE.md (§5 or a §-on observability) defining structured-log redaction of secret-shaped values and stating whether outbound provider context is secret-scanned/redacted before egress; if outbound scanning is out of scope, record that explicitly as an accepted limitation.

findings.finding: Decision needed on the strength of the renderer↔worker API auth boundary (drives the §5/§10 fix above): is a per-launch preload-injected session token over loopback TCP acceptable, or should V1 require a Unix domain socket (or randomized port + token) to defend against other local processes and browser-origin localhost access? This materially changes the §10 API transport contract and cannot be invented without the owner's risk tolerance for same-machine adversaries.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §10 lines 282-292 (transport unspecified beyond loopback tRPC + WS/SSE); THREAT_MODEL.md line 22 asserts 'origin/session checks' without a chosen mechanism; PRD §17 line 1007 specifies only loopback binding
findings.proposed_fix: Owner to choose the worker-API transport/auth posture (loopback TCP + session token vs Unix domain socket vs port+token). Record the choice in DECISIONS.md and pin it in ARCHITECTURE.md §10 before tasks-gen.
